summary: Gruber previews Apple's October 2008 MacBook lineup hours before the official announcement, confirming Engadget's leak of the redesigned 15-inch MacBook Pro and adding details about the new MacBooks and MacBook Airs. The new machines feature single-piece aluminum construction, glass multi-touch trackpads with no physical button, black-bordered displays, and Nvidia GPUs. He notes the 17-inch MacBook Pro is likely being phased out and that glossy displays are now the only option. Gruber predicts media coverage will fixate on the absence of a rumored sub-$1000 MacBook, despite those rumors having no credible sourcing. He observes that the new lineup may signal a shift away from CPU clock speed as the primary performance metric, with GPU capability becoming more important ahead of Snow Leopard's OpenCL.
key_points: The new MacBook Pro features a revolutionary glass trackpad that is itself a physical button—no separate button exists—along with single-piece aluminum construction and no physical latch
Apple is shipping only glossy displays on the new MacBook Pro, eliminating the matte option entirely
The 17-inch MacBook Pro is not being updated and Gruber suspects it's being phased out
The MacBook Pro ships with dual Nvidia GPUs (9400M and 9600M GT), a configuration that ties into Snow Leopard's OpenCL strategy for offloading computation to GPUs
The new $1299 MacBook has a slower CPU than the old $1299 model, potentially marking the beginning of the end of CPU clock speed as the dominant performance metric
money_quotes: The new MacBook Pro is not available with a matte-finish display. If you don't like glossy, you can suck it.
The single-button trackpad is gone. Which is to say there is no button at all.
What we're seeing may be the beginning of the end of CPU hertz as the rule-of-thumb metric for system performance.
Keep your eyes peeled for jackassery in post-event news coverage, much of which, I predict, will focus on the fact that none of these new machines sell for under $1299.
None of these '$800 new MacBook!' rumors came from anyone with any credibility, but that won't stop some people from holding it against Apple that they didn't pan out.
No more chintzy plastic trim along the edges and seams.
Engadget, which is reporting that the regular MacBooks aren't being revised, is wrong. They're not just being announced but should be shipping by the end of the week.
key_insight: Apple's 2008 MacBook redesign signals a fundamental shift in how laptop performance is measured, moving from CPU clock speed to GPU capability, while the media will predictably miss the story by obsessing over price points that were never credible.
